mapQuestions.question: Which part of the world is Laos in?
mapQuestions.answer: South-East Asia. Laos sits on mainland South-East Asia, surrounded by China, Vietnam, Cambodia, Thailand and Myanmar 🌏

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Laos?
mapQuestions.answer: Vientiane, on the Mekong River, right across the border from Thailand. It is one of the smallest, sleepiest capital cities in Asia.

mapQuestions.question: What is the only landlocked country in South-East Asia?
mapQuestions.answer: Laos. Every other South-East Asian country has a coastline — Laos is the only one with no ocean at all.

mapQuestions.question: What great river runs the length of Laos?
mapQuestions.answer: The Mekong. It flows nearly 1,900 km through Laos — more than any other country it touches.

mapQuestions.question: How much of Laos is forest or mountains?
mapQuestions.answer: Around 70% of the country is forested or mountainous. The flat parts are mostly along the Mekong, where the rice grows.

foods.name: Larb
foods.description: The national dish: chopped meat (chicken, pork or beef) cooked with lime juice, fish sauce, toasted rice powder, mint, coriander and chillies. Served warm or cool with sticky rice.

foods.name: Khao niaw (sticky rice)
foods.description: Steamed glutinous rice eaten with the hands. Roll a small ball, dip it in your dish, eat. Laotians eat more sticky rice per person than anyone else on Earth.

foods.name: Tam mak houng
foods.description: Green papaya salad: shredded unripe papaya pounded in a wooden mortar with lime, garlic, chillies, fish sauce and tomato. Spicy, sour, salty, sweet all at once.

foods.name: Khao jee paté
foods.description: A toasted Lao baguette stuffed with paté, pickled carrots, herbs and chilli — a leftover from 60 years of French colonial influence. Sold from carts at the side of the road.

foods.name: Or lam
foods.description: A Luang Prabang speciality: a hearty stew of buffalo or beef, lemongrass, dill and aubergine, often containing "sakhan" — a peppery vine root that numbs your tongue slightly.

foods.name: Lao coffee
foods.description: Strong dark coffee grown in the cool Bolaven Plateau, often brewed in a sock filter and served with sweet condensed milk at the bottom of the glass. Stir before drinking.

scavengerItems.label: A monk in saffron-orange robes walking with a begging bowl at dawn
A Lao baguette being eaten in the street (a French colonial leftover)
A bamboo basket of sticky rice being passed at a table
The brown muddy Mekong River, with longtail boats on it
A bamboo bridge crossing a stream or a paddy
A wat (Buddhist temple) with carved wooden doors and a gilded roof
A buffalo (or two) wading through a rice paddy
A woman weaving silk on a wooden loom under her stilt-house
Mist hanging in the mountains around Vang Vieng or Luang Prabang
A turquoise waterfall (Kuang Si is the most famous)
A "Beware of UXO" (unexploded ordnance) sign in the countryside
A "jumbo" three-wheel tuk-tuk loaded with people, fruit, or chickens

geoMatches.description: The capital, on the Mekong opposite Thailand. Tree-lined boulevards, sleepy by Asian standards. The towering golden Pha That Luang stupa is the national symbol.
A UNESCO-listed town where two rivers meet, ringed by misty mountains. Saffron-robed monks collect alms at dawn, French colonial buildings line the streets.
In Xieng Khouang province. Thousands of mysterious ancient stone jars, some 3 metres tall, scattered across fields. Around 2,500 years old. Nobody fully knows why they were carved.
A small town in central Laos surrounded by jagged karst limestone mountains. Rivers run through caves, kayakers and tubers float through paddies, hot-air balloons rise at dawn.
A high cool plateau in the south. Coffee plantations, tea, waterfalls cascading off the edge, and indigenous Mon-Khmer villages.
In the far south, where the Mekong braids out into a maze of small islands before flowing into Cambodia. Some of the last Irrawaddy river dolphins live here.

senses.smell: Sticky rice steaming in bamboo baskets, smoking fish on roadside grills, frangipani flowers in temple courtyards, Mekong river mist at dawn, fresh-roasted Lao coffee, woodsmoke from stilt houses…
senses.hear: Lao (very similar to Thai), gentle Buddhist chanting at dawn and dusk, "Sabaidee!", roosters everywhere, motorbike horns, slow river boats, drums from a temple festival…
senses.taste: Sweet sticky rice rolled into a ball, fiery larb with toasted rice, sharp-spicy-sour papaya salad, salty grilled river fish, dark Lao coffee with condensed milk pooling at the bottom…
senses.feel: Tropical humidity, cool blue waterfall water on hot skin, warm temple stones underfoot, soft sticky rice in your hand, gentle monsoon rain, fine river spray on the boat…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What is the alms ceremony?
stories.body: Every morning before sunrise in Luang Prabang, hundreds of orange-robed Buddhist monks walk silently through the streets in long single files, carrying their begging bowls. Local Buddhists kneel quietly on mats along the road and place small balls of sticky rice or other offerings into each bowl. The monks do not make eye contact, do not say thank you — they simply receive. It is a 700-year-old tradition still alive today. Tourists are welcome to watch quietly, sometimes participate (with respect: dress modestly, don't use flash, kneel below the monks). The monks eat only what they receive in alms, and only before noon — they don't eat at all for the rest of the day.

stories.question: Why is sticky rice such a big deal?
stories.body: Lao people eat more sticky rice per person than anyone else on Earth — around 145 kg per person per year. It is served in little woven bamboo baskets at every meal. The proper way to eat it: take a small handful with your right hand, press it into a ball, dip it into the dish (larb, jeow, soup), then eat. Sticky rice is not just food in Laos — it is part of religious ceremonies, weddings, and offerings. "Khao tom" (sticky rice in banana leaves) is left at temples and given to children as blessings. Lao people will tell you they "miss sticky rice" when they travel abroad, the way other people miss bread.

stories.question: Why is the Mekong River so important?
stories.body: The Mekong is one of the world's great rivers, and it runs the length of Laos for nearly 1,900 km. It is the country's lifeline: fish for food (the Mekong has more fish species than any river except the Amazon, around 1,200), transport, water for rice paddies, and rich silt deposited every monsoon to make the farmland fertile. The river floods predictably every year — Lao people plan their lives around it. Building hydropower dams on the Mekong (in China upstream, and now in Laos itself) is one of the biggest controversies of modern South-East Asia, affecting fishing communities and rice farmers thousands of miles downstream in Cambodia and Vietnam.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If one country builds a dam that helps its own people but hurts farmers downstream in other countries, who should decide?

stories.question: Why are there warning signs about unexploded bombs?
stories.body: From 1964 to 1973, during the Vietnam War, the United States secretly dropped more bombs on tiny Laos than were dropped on all of Europe in the whole of World War 2. The "Secret War" was never formally declared and was kept hidden from the American public for years. The US was trying to disrupt the Ho Chi Minh Trail, used by North Vietnam to move troops and supplies through Laos. Over 2 million tonnes of bombs were dropped — the equivalent of one planeload of bombs every 8 minutes, 24 hours a day, for 9 years. Around 30% of those bombs failed to explode at the time and still lie buried in the ground. They kill or maim around 50 Laotians every year, mostly farmers digging fields and children playing. Clearing them all will take generations. Laos is now the most-bombed country per person in history.

stories.question: How did a 600-year-old kingdom become a communist state?
stories.body: Laos was a kingdom for over 600 years, with its capital usually at Luang Prabang. The French colonised it in 1893 and ruled for 60 years, leaving behind baguettes, paté, croissants and the curving tarmac roads. After World War 2, the kingdom drifted into civil war. In 1975, after the end of the Vietnam War, the communist Pathet Lao movement took over the country, abolished the monarchy, and established the "Lao People's Democratic Republic" — still its official name. The king and royal family were sent to "re-education camps" where they died. Today Laos is one of the last remaining one-party communist states. Day-to-day life, though, is more about Buddhism, sticky rice, the Mekong and family than politics.

convoQuestions.question: Best Lao food you have tried so far?
Have you been brave enough to eat sticky rice with your hands? How did it go?
Did you catch the dawn alms ceremony? What did you make of the silence?
Mountains, river, monasteries or the slow countryside — which has felt most "Laos"?
Laos is famously the most chilled-out country in South-East Asia. Have you felt that pace?
75+ million unexploded bombs are still in the ground here. How do you live with that as a country?

wordSearch.hint: Lao foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Water buffalo
animals.description: Ploughing every rice paddy in the country. Often watched over by a small kid.

animals.name: Lao village dog
animals.description: Calm, sun-baked, ear-flopping dogs around every temple gate and market.

animals.name: Tokay gecko
animals.description: Loud "to-kay! to-kay!" call from house walls at night.

animals.name: Free-range cow
animals.description: Wandering along country roads — drivers stop and wait.

animals.name: Asian elephant
animals.description: At the Elephant Conservation Center in Sayaboury, where retired logging elephants live.

animals.name: Irrawaddy dolphin
animals.description: Around the 4,000 Islands in the south, on the Mekong near the Cambodian border.
